summary: Patrick McKenzie's 2014 year-in-review reveals his businesses grossed roughly $200,000 with $120,000 in profit across Appointment Reminder, Bingo Card Creator, and productized consulting. He candidly admits that Appointment Reminder, despite being his main focus since 2010, hasn't reached the $500k/year level he expected, largely because he doesn't find the problem space exciting. BCC declined 33% in revenue on autopilot while he struggled with burnout and health issues. His biggest regret is failing to ship a pre-sold conversion optimization course, which blocked his entire product pipeline. He moved his family from rural Ogaki to Tokyo, finally hired help for AR, and decided definitively against raising venture funding.
key_points: Appointment Reminder grew MRR 35% to ~$6,500/month but remains far below McKenzie's 2010 projection of $500k/year, largely because he finds the problem space boring and avoids work on it
Bingo Card Creator declined 33% in revenue to $33k but required less than 5 hours of work all year, yielding an implied ~$4,000/hour wage
McKenzie's failure to ship a pre-sold conversion optimization course became his biggest regret, blocking his entire productized consulting pipeline and causing substantial stress
He finally hired a sales rep and virtual assistant for AR, acknowledging he waited far too long to bring people on, and built sales automation software that reinvigorated his interest
He decided definitively against raising venture funding for AR because he doesn't want to commit 5+ years to a problem that's 7/10 fun on its best days
money_quotes: Is it weird to say that is distinct from actually wanting the money? I wouldn't mind a little bit more to establish more of a buffer for my family, but other than that, money qua money doesn't buy me anything I don't already have. It might as well be WoW gold.
Life is too short to do that. I certainly do not intend on devoting 90%+ of my career for the next five years to Appointment Reminder, so I won't take investment for it.

Peldi told me I should do a business which excites me. I told him that Appointment Reminder was a boring problem space but a great business, and that I'd find some challenge in it which was different than what I'd done with BCC.
I walked it into the bank and was shaking because I thought they'd shoot me for attempting a robbery. They didn't.
How can I trust you with my business long-term if you won't answer an easy question on day 1?
The $8.95 I paid for the domain name is probably the best ROI of anything I've ever bought.
The slip date shipped repeatedly — that spelling mistake was unintentional but I like it so much I'm leaving it in.
Some days I feel like I have a few mana pools of creative energy — one for programming, one for writing/speaking/podcasting, one for generic business stuff.
key_insight: Passion for the problem you're solving matters more than the business opportunity — without it, even a smart operator will chronically underinvest in a viable product.
